There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by [[Ono no Takamura]], the [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Mutsu Province|Ōshū Province]],{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of [[Kyoto]]. The shrine is mentioned by the [[Heian period]] poet [[Nōin]] during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Reizei]] (1045-1068) and during the [[Sengoku period]] was awarded an estate by local warlord [[Date Tanemune]]. It continued to be supported by the [[Date clan]] of [[Sendai Domain]] through the end of the [[Edo period]]. Following the start of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, [[Date Yoshimura]], but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] ==External links== *[http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288 Official site of the shrine] ==Notes== {{Commons Category|Takekoma-jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Iwanuma, Miyagi]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:Miyagi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects with bad categories]][[Category: 6]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]]{{Inari Faith}}

There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by [[Ono no Takamura]], the [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Mutsu Province|Ōshū Province]],{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of [[Kyoto]]. The shrine is mentioned by the [[Heian period]] poet [[Nōin]] during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Reizei]] (1045-1068) and during the [[Sengoku period]] was awarded an estate by local warlord [[Date Tanemune]]. It continued to be supported by the [[Date clan]] of [[Sendai Domain]] through the end of the [[Edo period]]. Following the start of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, [[Date Yoshimura]], but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' This festival is estimated to draw a quarter-million attendees.{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |title=Traditional Events |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Prefectural Government website |year=2004 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070220124655/http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |archivedate=2007-02-20 }} The shrine is also home to a horsemanship museum. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Takekoma Inari Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Ukanomitama]]| 倉稲魂神}}, traditionally identified as with Inari, and associated with agriculture, especially rice production. Secondary ''kami'' include {{nihongo|[[Uke Mochi]]| 保食神}}, the goddess of food, and {{nihongo|[[Wakumusubi]]| 稚産霊神}}, the god of the five cereals. There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by [[Ono no Takamura]], the [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Mutsu Province|Ōshū Province]],{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of [[Kyoto]]. The shrine is mentioned by the [[Heian period]] poet [[Nōin]] during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Reizei]] (1045-1068) and during the [[Sengoku period]] was awarded an estate by local warlord [[Date Tanemune]]. It continued to be supported by the [[Date clan]] of [[Sendai Domain]] through the end of the [[Edo period]]. Following the start of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, [[Date Yoshimura]], but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] ==External links== *[http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288 Official site of the shrine] ==Notes== {{Commons Category|Takekoma-jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Iwanuma, Miyagi]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:Miyagi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia bump with bad categories]][[Category: 6]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:Qq]]{{Izanami-firebirths}}[[Category:qq]] [[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]]{{Inari Faith}}

{{Infobox religious building | name = Takekoma Inari Jinja
竹駒稲荷神社 | image = Takekoma shrine.jpg | caption = Haiden of Takekoma Inari Jinja | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|38|6|18|N|140|51|44.4|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Inari Shrine]] | deity = | founded_by = | established = 842 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Inari-chō, Iwanuma-shi, Miyagi-ken, | website = {{URL|1=http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288|2=www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Inari Shrine'''|竹駒稲荷神社}} is a [[Shintō shrine]] in the city of [[Iwanuma, Miyagi|Iwanuma]] in [[Miyagi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is considered one of the three main shrines dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], and claims to be the second-oldest Inari shrine in Japan.Smyers, Karen Ann. The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press, 1999. 17-18. {{ISBN|0-8248-2102-5}} It was also referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Myojin'''|武隈明神}} Inari's traditional festival day is the first horse day (the sixth day) of the second month of the [[lunisolar calendar]]; in recent years, the shrine has celebrated the event on a Sunday in February or early March. This festival is estimated to draw a quarter-million attendees.{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |title=Traditional Events |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Prefectural Government website |year=2004 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070220124655/http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |archivedate=2007-02-20 }} The shrine is also home to a horsemanship museum. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Takekoma Inari Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Ukanomitama]]| 倉稲魂神}}, traditionally identified as with Inari, and associated with agriculture, especially rice production. Secondary ''kami'' include {{nihongo|[[Uke Mochi]]| 保食神}}, the goddess of food, and {{nihongo|[[Wakumusubi]]| 稚産霊神}}, the god of the five cereals. There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by [[Ono no Takamura]], the [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Mutsu Province|Ōshū Province]],{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of [[Kyoto]]. The shrine is mentioned by the [[Heian period]] poet [[Nōin]] during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Reizei]] (1045-1068) and during the [[Sengoku period]] was awarded an estate by local warlord [[Date Tanemune]]. It continued to be supported by the [[Date clan]] of [[Sendai Domain]] through the end of the [[Edo period]]. Following the start of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, [[Date Yoshimura]], but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] ==External links== *[http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288 Official site of the shrine] ==Notes== {{Commons Category|Takekoma-jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Iwanuma, Miyagi]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:qq]]{{Beppyo Shrines}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:qq]]{{Beppyo Shrines}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:Miyagi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:misc bump category]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category: 6]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:Qq]]{{Izanami-firebirths}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]]{{Inari Faith}}

The shrine is mentioned by the [[Heian period]] poet [[Nōin]] during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Reizei]] (1045-1068) and during the [[Sengoku period]] was awarded an estate by local warlord [[Date Tanemune]]. It continued to be supported by the [[Date clan]] of [[Sendai Domain]] through the end of the [[Edo period]]. Following the start of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, [[Date Yoshimura]], but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. {{Infobox religious building | name = Takekoma Inari Jinja
竹駒稲荷神社 | image = Takekoma shrine.jpg | caption = Haiden of Takekoma Inari Jinja | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|38|6|18|N|140|51|44.4|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Inari Shrine]] | deity = | founded_by = | established = 842 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Inari-chō, Iwanuma-shi, Miyagi-ken, | website = {{URL|1=http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288|2=www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Inari Shrine'''|竹駒稲荷神社}} is a [[Shintō shrine]] in the city of [[Iwanuma, Miyagi|Iwanuma]] in [[Miyagi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is considered one of the three main shrines dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], and claims to be the second-oldest Inari shrine in Japan.Smyers, Karen Ann. The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press, 1999. 17-18. {{ISBN|0-8248-2102-5}} It was also referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Myojin'''|武隈明神}} Inari's traditional festival day is the first horse day (the sixth day) of the second month of the [[lunisolar calendar]]; in recent years, the shrine has celebrated the event on a Sunday in February or early March. This festival is estimated to draw a quarter-million attendees.{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |title=Traditional Events |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Prefectural Government website |year=2004 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070220124655/http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |archivedate=2007-02-20 }} The shrine is also home to a horsemanship museum. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Takekoma Inari Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Ukanomitama]]| 倉稲魂神}}, traditionally identified as with Inari, and associated with agriculture, especially rice production. Secondary ''kami'' include {{nihongo|[[Uke Mochi]]| 保食神}}, the goddess of food, and {{nihongo|[[Wakumusubi]]| 稚産霊神}}, the god of the five cereals. There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by [[Ono no Takamura]], the [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Mutsu Province|Ōshū Province]],{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of [[Kyoto]]. The shrine is mentioned by the [[Heian period]] poet [[Nōin]] during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Reizei]] (1045-1068) and during the [[Sengoku period]] was awarded an estate by local warlord [[Date Tanemune]]. It continued to be supported by the [[Date clan]] of [[Sendai Domain]] through the end of the [[Edo period]]. Following the start of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, [[Date Yoshimura]], but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] ==External links== *[http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288 Official site of the shrine] ==Notes== {{Commons Category|Takekoma-jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Iwanuma, Miyagi]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:qq]]{{Beppyo Shrines}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:qq]]{{Beppyo Shrines}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:Miyagi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:misc bump Category]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category: 6]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:bump February 14, 2025 bump]] [[Category:Qq]]{{Izanami-firebirths}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]]{{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:New]]

There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by [[Ono no Takamura]], the [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Mutsu Province|Ōshū Province]],{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of [[Kyoto]]. The shrine is mentioned by the [[Heian period]] poet [[Nōin]] during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Reizei]] (1045-1068) and during the [[Sengoku period]] was awarded an estate by local warlord [[Date Tanemune]]. It continued to be supported by the [[Date clan]] of [[Sendai Domain]] through the end of the [[Edo period]]. Following the start of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, [[Date Yoshimura]], but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' {{Infobox religious building | name = Takekoma Inari Jinja
竹駒稲荷神社 | image = Takekoma shrine.jpg | caption = Haiden of Takekoma Inari Jinja | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|38|6|18|N|140|51|44.4|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Inari Shrine]] | deity = | founded_by = | established = 842 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Inari-chō, Iwanuma-shi, Miyagi-ken, | website = {{URL|1=http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288|2=www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Inari Shrine'''|竹駒稲荷神社}} is a [[Shintō shrine]] in the city of [[Iwanuma, Miyagi|Iwanuma]] in [[Miyagi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is considered one of the three main shrines dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], and claims to be the second-oldest Inari shrine in Japan.Smyers, Karen Ann. The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press, 1999. 17-18. {{ISBN|0-8248-2102-5}} It was also referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Myojin'''|武隈明神}} Inari's traditional festival day is the first horse day (the sixth day) of the second month of the [[lunisolar calendar]]; in recent years, the shrine has celebrated the event on a Sunday in February or early March. This festival is estimated to draw a quarter-million attendees.{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |title=Traditional Events |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Prefectural Government website |year=2004 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070220124655/http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |archivedate=2007-02-20 }} The shrine is also home to a horsemanship museum. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Takekoma Inari Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Ukanomitama]]| 倉稲魂神}}, traditionally identified as with Inari, and associated with agriculture, especially rice production. Secondary ''kami'' include {{nihongo|[[Uke Mochi]]| 保食神}}, the goddess of food, and {{nihongo|[[Wakumusubi]]| 稚産霊神}}, the god of the five cereals. There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by [[Ono no Takamura]], the [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Mutsu Province|Ōshū Province]],{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of [[Kyoto]]. The shrine is mentioned by the [[Heian period]] poet [[Nōin]] during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Reizei]] (1045-1068) and during the [[Sengoku period]] was awarded an estate by local warlord [[Date Tanemune]]. It continued to be supported by the [[Date clan]] of [[Sendai Domain]] through the end of the [[Edo period]]. Following the start of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, [[Date Yoshimura]], but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] ==External links== *[http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288 Official site of the shrine] ==Notes== {{Commons This festival is estimated to draw a quarter-million attendees.{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |title=Traditional Events |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Prefectural Government website |year=2004 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070220124655/http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |archivedate=2007-02-20 }} The shrine is also home to a horsemanship museum. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Takekoma Inari Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Ukanomitama]]| 倉稲魂神}}, traditionally identified as with Inari, and associated with agriculture, especially rice production. Secondary ''kami'' include {{nihongo|[[Uke Mochi]]| 保食神}}, the goddess of food, and {{nihongo|[[Wakumusubi]]| 稚産霊神}}, the god of the five cereals. There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by [[Ono no Takamura]], the [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Mutsu Province|Ōshū Province]],{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of [[Kyoto]]. The shrine is mentioned by the [[Heian period]] poet [[Nōin]] during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Reizei]] (1045-1068) and during the [[Sengoku period]] was awarded an estate by local warlord [[Date Tanemune]]. It continued to be supported by the [[Date clan]] of [[Sendai Domain]] through the end of the [[Edo period]]. Following the start of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, [[Date Yoshimura]], but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] ==External links== *[http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288 Official site of the shrine] ==Notes== {{Commons {{Infobox religious building | name = Takekoma Inari Jinja
竹駒稲荷神社 | image = Takekoma shrine.jpg | caption = Haiden of Takekoma Inari Jinja | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|38|6|18|N|140|51|44.4|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Inari Shrine]] | deity = | founded_by = | established = 842 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Inari-chō, Iwanuma-shi, Miyagi-ken, | website = {{URL|1=http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288|2=www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Inari Shrine'''|竹駒稲荷神社}} is a [[Shintō shrine]] in the city of [[Iwanuma, Miyagi|Iwanuma]] in [[Miyagi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is considered one of the three main shrines dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], and claims to be the second-oldest Inari shrine in Japan.Smyers, Karen Ann. The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press, 1999. 17-18. {{ISBN|0-8248-2102-5}} It was also referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Myojin'''|武隈明神}} Inari's traditional festival day is the first horse day (the sixth day) of the second month of the [[lunisolar calendar]]; in recent years, the shrine has celebrated the event on a Sunday in February or early March. This festival is estimated to draw a quarter-million attendees.{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |title=Traditional Events |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Prefectural Government website |year=2004 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070220124655/http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |archivedate=2007-02-20 }} The shrine is also home to a horsemanship museum. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Takekoma Inari Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Ukanomitama]]| 倉稲魂神}}, traditionally identified as with Inari, and associated with agriculture, especially rice production. Secondary ''kami'' include {{nihongo|[[Uke Mochi]]| 保食神}}, the goddess of food, and {{nihongo|[[Wakumusubi]]| 稚産霊神}}, the god of the five cereals. There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by [[Ono no Takamura]], the [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Mutsu Province|Ōshū Province]],{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of [[Kyoto]]. The shrine is mentioned by the [[Heian period]] poet [[Nōin]] during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Reizei]] (1045-1068) and during the [[Sengoku period]] was awarded an estate by local warlord [[Date Tanemune]]. It continued to be supported by the [[Date clan]] of [[Sendai Domain]] through the end of the [[Edo period]]. Following the start of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, [[Date Yoshimura]], but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] ==External links== *[http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288 Official site of the shrine] ==Notes== {{Commons {{Infobox religious building | name = Takekoma Inari Jinja
竹駒稲荷神社 | image = Takekoma shrine.jpg | caption = Haiden of Takekoma Inari Jinja | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|38|6|18|N|140|51|44.4|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Inari Shrine]] | deity = | founded_by = | established = 842 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Inari-chō, Iwanuma-shi, Miyagi-ken, | website = {{URL|1=http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288|2=www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Inari Shrine'''|竹駒稲荷神社}} is a [[Shintō shrine]] in the city of [[Iwanuma, Miyagi|Iwanuma]] in [[Miyagi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is considered one of the three main shrines dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], and claims to be the second-oldest Inari shrine in Japan.Smyers, Karen Ann. The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press, 1999. 17-18. {{ISBN|0-8248-2102-5}} It was also referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Myojin'''|武隈明神}} Inari's traditional festival day is the first horse day (the sixth day) of the second month of the [[lunisolar calendar]]; in recent years, the shrine has celebrated the event on a Sunday in February or early March. This festival is estimated to draw a quarter-million attendees.{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |title=Traditional Events |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Prefectural Government website |year=2004 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070220124655/http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |archivedate=2007-02-20 }} The shrine is also home to a horsemanship museum. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Takekoma Inari Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Ukanomitama]]| 倉稲魂神}}, traditionally identified as with Inari, and associated with agriculture, especially rice production. Secondary ''kami'' include {{nihongo|[[Uke Mochi]]| 保食神}}, the goddess of food, and {{nihongo|[[Wakumusubi]]| 稚産霊神}}, the god of the five cereals. There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by [[Ono no Takamura]], the [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Mutsu Province|Ōshū Province]],{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of [[Kyoto]]. The shrine is mentioned by the [[Heian period]] poet [[Nōin]] during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Reizei]] (1045-1068) and during the [[Sengoku period]] was awarded an estate by local warlord [[Date Tanemune]]. It continued to be supported by the [[Date clan]] of [[Sendai Domain]] through the end of the [[Edo period]]. Following the start of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, [[Date Yoshimura]], but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] ==External links== *[http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288 Official site of the shrine] ==Notes== {{Commons There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by [[Ono no Takamura]], the [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Mutsu Province|Ōshū Province]],{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of [[Kyoto]]. The shrine is mentioned by the [[Heian period]] poet [[Nōin]] during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Reizei]] (1045-1068) and during the [[Sengoku period]] was awarded an estate by local warlord [[Date Tanemune]]. It continued to be supported by the [[Date clan]] of [[Sendai Domain]] through the end of the [[Edo period]]. Following the start of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, [[Date Yoshimura]], but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' {{Infobox religious building | name = Takekoma Inari Jinja

It is considered one of the three main shrines dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], and claims to be the second-oldest Inari shrine in Japan.Smyers, Karen Ann. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press, 1999. 17-18. {{ISBN|0-8248-2102-5}} It was also referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Myojin'''|武隈明神}} Inari's traditional festival day is the first horse day (the sixth day) of the second month of the [[lunisolar calendar]]; in recent years, the shrine has celebrated the event on a Sunday in February or early March. This festival is estimated to draw a quarter-million attendees.{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |title=Traditional Events |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Prefectural Government website |year=2004 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070220124655/http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |archivedate=2007-02-20 }} The shrine is also home to a horsemanship museum. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Takekoma Inari Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Ukanomitama]]| 倉稲魂神}}, traditionally identified as with Inari, and associated with agriculture, especially rice production. There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by [[Ono no Takamura]], the [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Mutsu Province|Ōshū Province]],{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of [[Kyoto]]. The shrine is mentioned by the [[Heian period]] poet [[Nōin]] during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Reizei]] (1045-1068) and during the [[Sengoku period]] was awarded an estate by local warlord [[Date Tanemune]]. It continued to be supported by the [[Date clan]] of [[Sendai Domain]] through the end of the [[Edo period]]. Following the start of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, [[Date Yoshimura]], but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' {{Infobox religious building | name = Takekoma Inari Jinja
竹駒稲荷神社 | image = Takekoma shrine.jpg | caption = Haiden of Takekoma Inari Jinja | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|38|6|18|N|140|51|44.4|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Inari Shrine]] | deity = | founded_by = | established = 842 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Inari-chō, Iwanuma-shi, Miyagi-ken, | website = {{URL|1=http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288|2=www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Inari Shrine'''|竹駒稲荷神社}} is a [[Shintō shrine]] in the city of [[Iwanuma, Miyagi|Iwanuma]] in [[Miyagi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is considered one of the three main shrines dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], and claims to be the second-oldest Inari shrine in Japan.Smyers, Karen Ann. The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press, 1999. 17-18. {{ISBN|0-8248-2102-5}} It was also referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Myojin'''|武隈明神}} Inari's traditional festival day is the first horse day (the sixth day) of the second month of the [[lunisolar calendar]]; in recent years, the shrine has celebrated the event on a Sunday in February or early March. This festival is estimated to draw a quarter-million attendees.{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |title=Traditional Events |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Prefectural Government website |year=2004 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070220124655/http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |archivedate=2007-02-20 }} The shrine is also home to a horsemanship museum. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Takekoma Inari Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Ukanomitama]]| 倉稲魂神}}, traditionally identified as with Inari, and associated with agriculture, especially rice production. Secondary ''kami'' include {{nihongo|[[Uke Mochi]]| 保食神}}, the goddess of food, and {{nihongo|[[Wakumusubi]]| 稚産霊神}}, the god of the five cereals. There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by [[Ono no Takamura]], the [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Mutsu Province|Ōshū Province]],{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of [[Kyoto]]. The shrine is mentioned by the [[Heian period]] poet [[Nōin]] during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Reizei]] (1045-1068) and during the [[Sengoku period]] was awarded an estate by local warlord [[Date Tanemune]]. It continued to be supported by the [[Date clan]] of [[Sendai Domain]] through the end of the [[Edo period]]. Following the start of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, [[Date Yoshimura]], but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] ==External links== *[http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288 Official site of the shrine] ==Notes== {{Commons 竹駒稲荷神社 | image = Takekoma shrine.jpg | caption = Haiden of Takekoma Inari Jinja | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|38|6|18|N|140|51|44.4|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Inari Shrine]] | deity = | founded_by = | established = 842 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Inari-chō, Iwanuma-shi, Miyagi-ken, | website = {{URL|1=http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288|2=www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Inari Shrine'''|竹駒稲荷神社}} is a [[Shintō shrine]] in the city of [[Iwanuma, Miyagi|Iwanuma]] in [[Miyagi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is considered one of the three main shrines dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], and claims to be the second-oldest Inari shrine in Japan.Smyers, Karen Ann. The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press, 1999. 17-18. {{ISBN|0-8248-2102-5}} It was also referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Myojin'''|武隈明神}} Inari's traditional festival day is the first horse day (the sixth day) of the second month of the [[lunisolar calendar]]; in recent years, the shrine has celebrated the event on a Sunday in February or early March. This festival is estimated to draw a quarter-million attendees.{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |title=Traditional Events |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Prefectural Government website |year=2004 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070220124655/http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |archivedate=2007-02-20 }} The shrine is also home to a horsemanship museum. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Takekoma Inari Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Ukanomitama]]| 倉稲魂神}}, traditionally identified as with Inari, and associated with agriculture, especially rice production. Secondary ''kami'' include {{nihongo|[[Uke Mochi]]| 保食神}}, the goddess of food, and {{nihongo|[[Wakumusubi]]| 稚産霊神}}, the god of the five cereals. There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by [[Ono no Takamura]], the [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Mutsu Province|Ōshū Province]],{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of [[Kyoto]]. The shrine is mentioned by the [[Heian period]] poet [[Nōin]] during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Reizei]] (1045-1068) and during the [[Sengoku period]] was awarded an estate by local warlord [[Date Tanemune]]. It continued to be supported by the [[Date clan]] of [[Sendai Domain]] through the end of the [[Edo period]]. Following the start of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, [[Date Yoshimura]], but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] ==External links== *[http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288 Official site of the shrine] ==Notes== {{Commons It continued to be supported by the [[Date clan]] of [[Sendai Domain]] through the end of the [[Edo period]]. Following the start of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, [[Date Yoshimura]], but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' {{Infobox religious building | name = Takekoma Inari Jinja
竹駒稲荷神社 | image = Takekoma shrine.jpg | caption = Haiden of Takekoma Inari Jinja | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|38|6|18|N|140|51|44.4|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Inari Shrine]] | deity = | founded_by = | established = 842 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Inari-chō, Iwanuma-shi, Miyagi-ken, | website = {{URL|1=http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288|2=www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Inari Shrine'''|竹駒稲荷神社}} is a [[Shintō shrine]] in the city of [[Iwanuma, Miyagi|Iwanuma]] in [[Miyagi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is considered one of the three main shrines dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], and claims to be the second-oldest Inari shrine in Japan.Smyers, Karen Ann. The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press, 1999. 17-18. {{ISBN|0-8248-2102-5}} It was also referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Myojin'''|武隈明神}} Inari's traditional festival day is the first horse day (the sixth day) of the second month of the [[lunisolar calendar]]; in recent years, the shrine has celebrated the event on a Sunday in February or early March. This festival is estimated to draw a quarter-million attendees.{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |title=Traditional Events |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Prefectural Government website |year=2004 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070220124655/http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |archivedate=2007-02-20 }} The shrine is also home to a horsemanship museum. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Takekoma Inari Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Ukanomitama]]| 倉稲魂神}}, traditionally identified as with Inari, and associated with agriculture, especially rice production. Secondary ''kami'' include {{nihongo|[[Uke Mochi]]| 保食神}}, the goddess of food, and {{nihongo|[[Wakumusubi]]| 稚産霊神}}, the god of the five cereals. There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by [[Ono no Takamura]], the [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Mutsu Province|Ōshū Province]],{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of [[Kyoto]]. The shrine is mentioned by the [[Heian period]] poet [[Nōin]] during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Reizei]] (1045-1068) and during the [[Sengoku period]] was awarded an estate by local warlord [[Date Tanemune]]. It continued to be supported by the [[Date clan]] of [[Sendai Domain]] through the end of the [[Edo period]]. Following the start of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, [[Date Yoshimura]], but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] ==External links== *[http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288 Official site of the shrine] ==Notes== {{Commons {{Infobox religious building | name = Takekoma Inari Jinja
竹駒稲荷神社 | image = Takekoma shrine.jpg | caption = Haiden of Takekoma Inari Jinja | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|38|6|18|N|140|51|44.4|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Inari Shrine]] | deity = | founded_by = | established = 842 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Inari-chō, Iwanuma-shi, Miyagi-ken, | website = {{URL|1=http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288|2=www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Inari Shrine'''|竹駒稲荷神社}} is a [[Shintō shrine]] in the city of [[Iwanuma, Miyagi|Iwanuma]] in [[Miyagi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is considered one of the three main shrines dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], and claims to be the second-oldest Inari shrine in Japan.Smyers, Karen Ann. The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press, 1999. 17-18. {{ISBN|0-8248-2102-5}} It was also referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Myojin'''|武隈明神}} Inari's traditional festival day is the first horse day (the sixth day) of the second month of the [[lunisolar calendar]]; in recent years, the shrine has celebrated the event on a Sunday in February or early March. This festival is estimated to draw a quarter-million attendees.{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |title=Traditional Events |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Prefectural Government website |year=2004 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070220124655/http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |archivedate=2007-02-20 }} The shrine is also home to a horsemanship museum. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Takekoma Inari Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Ukanomitama]]| 倉稲魂神}}, traditionally identified as with Inari, and associated with agriculture, especially rice production. Secondary ''kami'' include {{nihongo|[[Uke Mochi]]| 保食神}}, the goddess of food, and {{nihongo|[[Wakumusubi]]| 稚産霊神}}, the god of the five cereals. There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by [[Ono no Takamura]], the [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Mutsu Province|Ōshū Province]],{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of [[Kyoto]]. The shrine is mentioned by the [[Heian period]] poet [[Nōin]] during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Reizei]] (1045-1068) and during the [[Sengoku period]] was awarded an estate by local warlord [[Date Tanemune]]. It continued to be supported by the [[Date clan]] of [[Sendai Domain]] through the end of the [[Edo period]]. Following the start of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, [[Date Yoshimura]], but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] ==External links== *[http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288 Official site of the shrine] ==Notes== {{Commons Category|Takekoma-jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] The shrine is mentioned by the [[Heian period]] poet [[Nōin]] during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Reizei]] (1045-1068) and during the [[Sengoku period]] was awarded an estate by local warlord [[Date Tanemune]]. It continued to be supported by the [[Date clan]] of [[Sendai Domain]] through the end of the [[Edo period]]. Following the start of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, [[Date Yoshimura]], but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press, 1999. 17-18. {{ISBN|0-8248-2102-5}} It was also referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Myojin'''|武隈明神}} Inari's traditional festival day is the first horse day (the sixth day) of the second month of the [[lunisolar calendar]]; in recent years, the shrine has celebrated the event on a Sunday in February or early March. This festival is estimated to draw a quarter-million attendees.{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |title=Traditional Events |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Prefectural Government website |year=2004 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070220124655/http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |archivedate=2007-02-20 }} The shrine is also home to a horsemanship museum. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Takekoma Inari Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Ukanomitama]]| 倉稲魂神}}, traditionally identified as with Inari, and associated with agriculture, especially rice production. Secondary ''kami'' include {{nihongo|[[Uke Mochi]]| 保食神}}, the goddess of food, and {{nihongo|[[Wakumusubi]]| 稚産霊神}}, the god of the five cereals. There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by [[Ono no Takamura]], the [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Mutsu Province|Ōshū Province]],{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of [[Kyoto]]. The shrine is mentioned by the [[Heian period]] poet [[Nōin]] during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Reizei]] (1045-1068) and during the [[Sengoku period]] was awarded an estate by local warlord [[Date Tanemune]]. It continued to be supported by the [[Date clan]] of [[Sendai Domain]] through the end of the [[Edo period]]. Following the start of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, [[Date Yoshimura]], but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] ==External links== *[http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288 Official site of the shrine] ==Notes== {{Commons {{Infobox religious building | name = Takekoma Inari Jinja
竹駒稲荷神社 | image = Takekoma shrine.jpg | caption = Haiden of Takekoma Inari Jinja | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|38|6|18|N|140|51|44.4|E|source:jawiki_region:JP_scale:20000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Inari Shrine]] | deity = | founded_by = | established = 842 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Inari-chō, Iwanuma-shi, Miyagi-ken, | website = {{URL|1=http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288|2=www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} The {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Inari Shrine'''|竹駒稲荷神社}} is a [[Shintō shrine]] in the city of [[Iwanuma, Miyagi|Iwanuma]] in [[Miyagi Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is considered one of the three main shrines dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' [[Inari (mythology)|Inari]], and claims to be the second-oldest Inari shrine in Japan.Smyers, Karen Ann. The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press, 1999. 17-18. {{ISBN|0-8248-2102-5}} It was also referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Myojin'''|武隈明神}} Inari's traditional festival day is the first horse day (the sixth day) of the second month of the [[lunisolar calendar]]; in recent years, the shrine has celebrated the event on a Sunday in February or early March. This festival is estimated to draw a quarter-million attendees.{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |title=Traditional Events |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Prefectural Government website |year=2004 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070220124655/http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |archivedate=2007-02-20 }} The shrine is also home to a horsemanship museum. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Takekoma Inari Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Ukanomitama]]| 倉稲魂神}}, traditionally identified as with Inari, and associated with agriculture, especially rice production. Secondary ''kami'' include {{nihongo|[[Uke Mochi]]| 保食神}}, the goddess of food, and {{nihongo|[[Wakumusubi]]| 稚産霊神}}, the god of the five cereals. There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by [[Ono no Takamura]], the [[Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[Mutsu Province|Ōshū Province]],{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of [[Kyoto]]. The shrine is mentioned by the [[Heian period]] poet [[Nōin]] during the reign of [[Emperor Go-Reizei]] (1045-1068) and during the [[Sengoku period]] was awarded an estate by local warlord [[Date Tanemune]]. It continued to be supported by the [[Date clan]] of [[Sendai Domain]] through the end of the [[Edo period]]. Following the start of [[State Shinto]] in the [[Meiji period]], the shrine was ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, [[Date Yoshimura]], but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] ==External links== *[http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288 Official site of the shrine] ==Notes== {{Commons Category|Takekoma-jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} ]] {{Izanami-firebirths}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Iwanuma, Miyagi]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Miyagi Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Takekoma Inari Shrine]] [[Category:misc bump Category]] [[Category: 6]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:C111]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]] [[sn:Takekoma Inari Shrine]]
