[[sn:commons:Category:Takekoma-jinja]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[fr:Takekoma Inari-jinja]][[ja:竹駒神社]][[uk:Святилище Такекома]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q521319]] Wikidata name: Takekoma Inari Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:47, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takekoma Inari Jinja
竹駒稲荷神社 | image = File:Takekoma shrine.jpg | caption = Haiden of Takekoma Inari Jinja | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|38.105|N|140.862|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]] | deity ={{ill|Ukanomitama|WD=Q3080728}} | founded_by = | established = 842 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Inari-chō, Iwanuma-shi, Miyagi-ken, | website = {{URL|1=http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288|2=www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Iwanuma|WD=Q518203}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Inari Shrine'''|竹駒稲荷神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[:en:Iwanuma, Miyagi|Iwanuma]] in [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is considered one of the three main shrines dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]], and claims to be the second-oldest Inari shrine in Japan.Smyers, Karen Ann. The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press, 1999. 17-18. {{ISBN|0-8248-2102-5}} It was also referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Myojin'''|武隈明神}} Inari's traditional festival day is the first horse day (the sixth day) of the second month of the [[:en:en:Lunisolar calendar|en:Lunisolar calendar]]; in recent years, the shrine has celebrated the event on a Sunday in February or early March. This festival is estimated to draw a quarter-million attendees.{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |title=Traditional Events |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Prefectural Government website |year=2004 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070220124655/http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |archivedate=2007-02-20 }} The shrine is also home to a horsemanship museum. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Takekoma Inari Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Ukanomitama]]| 倉稲魂神}}, traditionally identified as with Inari, and associated with agriculture, especially rice production. Secondary ''kami'' include {{nihongo|[[:en:Ukemochi|Ukemochi]]| 保食神}}, the goddess of food, and {{nihongo|[[Wakumusubi]]| 稚産霊神}}, the god of the five cereals. There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by [[:en:Ono no Takamura|Ono no Takamura]], the [[:en:Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[:en:Mutsu Province|Ōshū Province]],{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[:en:Fushimi Inari Shrine|Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. The shrine is mentioned by the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] poet [[:en:Nōin|Nōin]] during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Reizei|Emperor Go-Reizei]] (1045-1068) and during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] was awarded an estate by local warlord [[:en:Date Tanemune|Date Tanemune]]. It continued to be supported by the [[:en:Date clan|Date clan]] of [[:en:Sendai Domain|Sendai Domain]] through the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Following the start of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the shrine was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, [[:en:Date Yoshimura|Date Yoshimura]], but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[:en:Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] ==External links== *[http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288 Official site of the shrine] ==Notes== {{Commons Category|Takekoma-jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Izanami-firebirths}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] }} [[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Takekoma-jinja}} [[fr:Takekoma Inari-jinja]] [[ja:竹駒神社]] [[uk:Святилище Такекома]] {{wikidata link|Q521319}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

This festival is estimated to draw a quarter-million attendees.{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |title=Traditional Events |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Prefectural Government website |year=2004 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070220124655/http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |archivedate=2007-02-20 }} The shrine is also home to a horsemanship museum. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Takekoma Inari Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Ukanomitama]]| 倉稲魂神}}, traditionally identified as with Inari, and associated with agriculture, especially rice production. Secondary ''kami'' include {{nihongo|[[:en:Ukemochi|Ukemochi]]| 保食神}}, the goddess of food, and {{nihongo|[[Wakumusubi]]| 稚産霊神}}, the god of the five cereals. There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by [[:en:Ono no Takamura|Ono no Takamura]], the [[:en:Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[:en:Mutsu Province|Ōshū Province]],{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[:en:Fushimi Inari Shrine|Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. The shrine is mentioned by the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] poet [[:en:Nōin|Nōin]] during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Reizei|Emperor Go-Reizei]] (1045-1068) and during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] was awarded an estate by local warlord [[:en:Date Tanemune|Date Tanemune]]. It continued to be supported by the [[:en:Date clan|Date clan]] of [[:en:Sendai Domain|Sendai Domain]] through the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Following the start of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the shrine was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, [[:en:Date Yoshimura|Date Yoshimura]], but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[:en:Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] ==External links== *[http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288 Official site of the shrine] ==Notes== {{Commons Category|Takekoma-jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Izanami-firebirths}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] }} [[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Takekoma-jinja}} [[fr:Takekoma Inari-jinja]] [[ja:竹駒神社]] [[uk:Святилище Такекома]] {{wikidata link|Q521319}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[commons:Category:Takekoma-jinja]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[fr:Takekoma Inari-jinja]][[ja:竹駒神社]][[uk:Святилище Такекома]] [[sn:commons:Category:Takekoma-jinja]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[fr:Takekoma Inari-jinja]][[ja:竹駒神社]][[uk:Святилище Такекома]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q521319]] Wikidata name: Takekoma Inari Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:47, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takekoma Inari Jinja
竹駒稲荷神社 | image = File:Takekoma shrine.jpg | caption = Haiden of Takekoma Inari Jinja | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|38.105|N|140.862|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]] | deity ={{ill|Ukanomitama|WD=Q3080728}} | founded_by = | established = 842 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Inari-chō, Iwanuma-shi, Miyagi-ken, | website = {{URL|1=http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288|2=www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Iwanuma|WD=Q518203}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Inari Shrine'''|竹駒稲荷神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[:en:Iwanuma, Miyagi|Iwanuma]] in [[:en:Miyagi Prefecture|Miyagi Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It is considered one of the three main shrines dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]], and claims to be the second-oldest Inari shrine in Japan.Smyers, Karen Ann. The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press, 1999. 17-18. {{ISBN|0-8248-2102-5}} It was also referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Myojin'''|武隈明神}} Inari's traditional festival day is the first horse day (the sixth day) of the second month of the [[:en:en:Lunisolar calendar|en:Lunisolar calendar]]; in recent years, the shrine has celebrated the event on a Sunday in February or early March. This festival is estimated to draw a quarter-million attendees.{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |title=Traditional Events |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Prefectural Government website |year=2004 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070220124655/http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |archivedate=2007-02-20 }} The shrine is also home to a horsemanship museum. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Takekoma Inari Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Ukanomitama]]| 倉稲魂神}}, traditionally identified as with Inari, and associated with agriculture, especially rice production. Secondary ''kami'' include {{nihongo|[[:en:Ukemochi|Ukemochi]]| 保食神}}, the goddess of food, and {{nihongo|[[Wakumusubi]]| 稚産霊神}}, the god of the five cereals. There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by [[:en:Ono no Takamura|Ono no Takamura]], the [[:en:Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[:en:Mutsu Province|Ōshū Province]],{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[:en:Fushimi Inari Shrine|Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. The shrine is mentioned by the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] poet [[:en:Nōin|Nōin]] during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Reizei|Emperor Go-Reizei]] (1045-1068) and during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] was awarded an estate by local warlord [[:en:Date Tanemune|Date Tanemune]]. It continued to be supported by the [[:en:Date clan|Date clan]] of [[:en:Sendai Domain|Sendai Domain]] through the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Following the start of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the shrine was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, [[:en:Date Yoshimura|Date Yoshimura]], but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[:en:Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] ==External links== *[http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288 Official site of the shrine] ==Notes== {{Commons Category|Takekoma-jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Izanami-firebirths}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] }} [[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Takekoma-jinja}} [[fr:Takekoma Inari-jinja]] [[ja:竹駒神社]] [[uk:Святилище Такекома]] {{wikidata link|Q521319}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

Secondary ''kami'' include {{nihongo|[[:en:Ukemochi|Ukemochi]]| 保食神}}, the goddess of food, and {{nihongo|[[Wakumusubi]]| 稚産霊神}}, the god of the five cereals. There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by [[:en:Ono no Takamura|Ono no Takamura]], the [[:en:Kokushi (official)|''kokushi'']] of [[:en:Mutsu Province|Ōshū Province]],{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[:en:Fushimi Inari Shrine|Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. The shrine is mentioned by the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] poet [[:en:Nōin|Nōin]] during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Go-Reizei|Emperor Go-Reizei]] (1045-1068) and during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]] was awarded an estate by local warlord [[:en:Date Tanemune|Date Tanemune]]. It continued to be supported by the [[:en:Date clan|Date clan]] of [[:en:Sendai Domain|Sendai Domain]] through the end of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. Following the start of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], the shrine was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, [[:en:Date Yoshimura|Date Yoshimura]], but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * [[:en:Karen Ann Smyers|Smyers, Karen Ann]]. revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Takekoma-jinja]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[fr:Takekoma Inari-jinja]][[ja:竹駒神社]][[uk:Святилище Такекома]] [[co:commons:Category:Takekoma-jinja]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[fr:Takekoma Inari-jinja]][[ja:竹駒神社]][[uk:Святилище Такекома]] [[sn:commons:Category:Takekoma-jinja]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[fr:Takekoma Inari-jinja]][[ja:竹駒神社]][[uk:Святилище Такекома]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q521319]] Wikidata name: Takekoma Inari Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:47, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takekoma Inari Jinja
竹駒稲荷神社 | image = File:Takekoma shrine.jpg | caption = Haiden of Takekoma Inari Jinja | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|38.105|N|140.862|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine}} | deity ={{ill|Ukanomitama|WD=Q3080728}} | founded_by = | established = 842 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Inari-chō, Iwanuma-shi, Miyagi-ken, | website = {{URL|1=http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288|2=www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Iwanuma|WD=Q518203}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Inari Shrine'''|竹駒稲荷神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the city of [[:en:Iwanuma, Miyagi|Iwanuma]] in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|WD=Q47896|lt=Miyagi Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It is considered one of the three main shrines dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari}}, and claims to be the second-oldest Inari shrine in Japan.Smyers, Karen Ann. The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press, 1999. 17-18. {{ISBN|0-8248-2102-5}} It was also referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Myojin'''|武隈明神}} Inari's traditional festival day is the first horse day (the sixth day) of the second month of the {{ill|en:Lunisolar calendar|en|en:Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=en:Lunisolar calendar}}; in recent years, the shrine has celebrated the event on a Sunday in February or early March. This festival is estimated to draw a quarter-million attendees.{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |title=Traditional Events |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Prefectural Government website |year=2004 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070220124655/http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |archivedate=2007-02-20 }} The shrine is also home to a horsemanship museum. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Takekoma Inari Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Ukanomitama]]| 倉稲魂神}}, traditionally identified as with Inari, and associated with agriculture, especially rice production. Secondary ''kami'' include {{nihongo|{{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi}}| 保食神}}, the goddess of food, and {{nihongo|[[Wakumusubi]]| 稚産霊神}}, the god of the five cereals. There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by {{ill|Ono no Takamura|en|Ono no Takamura|WD=Q1139928|lt=Ono no Takamura}}, the {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=''kokushi''}} of {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Ōshū Province}},{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[:en:Fushimi Inari Shrine|Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}. The shrine is mentioned by the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} poet {{ill|Nōin|en|Nōin|WD=Q3074648|lt=Nōin}} during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Reizei|en|Emperor Go-Reizei|WD=Q356060|lt=Emperor Go-Reizei}} (1045-1068) and during the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} was awarded an estate by local warlord {{ill|Date Tanemune|en|Date Tanemune|WD=Q5227551|lt=Date Tanemune}}. It continued to be supported by the {{ill|Date clan|en|Date clan|WD=Q1151061|lt=Date clan}} of {{ill|Sendai Domain|en|Sendai Domain|WD=Q1062182|lt=Sendai Domain}} through the end of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. Following the start of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the shrine was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, {{ill|Date Yoshimura|en|Date Yoshimura|WD=Q116151|lt=Date Yoshimura}}, but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * {{ill|Karen Ann Smyers|en|Karen Ann Smyers|WD=Q6369485|lt=Smyers, Karen Ann}}. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] ==External links== *[http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288 Official site of the shrine] ==Notes== {{Commons Category|Takekoma-jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Izanami-firebirths}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]] {{Commons category|Takekoma-jinja}} [[fr:Takekoma Inari-jinja]] [[ja:竹駒神社]] [[uk:Святилище Такекома]] {{wikidata link|Q521319}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] ==External links== *[http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288 Official site of the shrine] ==Notes== {{Commons Category|Takekoma-jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Izanami-firebirths}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]] {{Commons category|Takekoma-jinja}} [[fr:Takekoma Inari-jinja]] [[ja:竹駒神社]] [[uk:Святилище Такекома]] {{wikidata link|Q521319}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[co:commons:Category:Takekoma-jinja]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[fr:Takekoma Inari-jinja]][[ja:竹駒神社]][[uk:Святилище Такекома]] [[co:commons:Category:Takekoma-jinja]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[fr:Takekoma Inari-jinja]][[ja:竹駒神社]][[uk:Святилище Такекома]] [[sn:commons:Category:Takekoma-jinja]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[fr:Takekoma Inari-jinja]][[ja:竹駒神社]][[uk:Святилище Такекома]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q521319]] Wikidata name: Takekoma Inari Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:47, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takekoma Inari Jinja
竹駒稲荷神社 | image = File:Takekoma shrine.jpg | caption = Haiden of Takekoma Inari Jinja | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|38.105|N|140.862|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine}} | deity ={{ill|Ukanomitama|WD=Q3080728}} | founded_by = | established = 842 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Inari-chō, Iwanuma-shi, Miyagi-ken, | website = {{URL|1=http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288|2=www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Iwanuma|WD=Q518203}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Inari Shrine'''|竹駒稲荷神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the city of [[:en:Iwanuma, Miyagi|Iwanuma]] in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|WD=Q47896|lt=Miyagi Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It is considered one of the three main shrines dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari}}, and claims to be the second-oldest Inari shrine in Japan.Smyers, Karen Ann. The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press, 1999. 17-18. {{ISBN|0-8248-2102-5}} It was also referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Myojin'''|武隈明神}} Inari's traditional festival day is the first horse day (the sixth day) of the second month of the {{ill|en:Lunisolar calendar|en|en:Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=en:Lunisolar calendar}}; in recent years, the shrine has celebrated the event on a Sunday in February or early March. This festival is estimated to draw a quarter-million attendees.{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |title=Traditional Events |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Prefectural Government website |year=2004 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070220124655/http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |archivedate=2007-02-20 }} The shrine is also home to a horsemanship museum. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Takekoma Inari Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Ukanomitama]]| 倉稲魂神}}, traditionally identified as with Inari, and associated with agriculture, especially rice production. Secondary ''kami'' include {{nihongo|{{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi}}| 保食神}}, the goddess of food, and {{nihongo|[[Wakumusubi]]| 稚産霊神}}, the god of the five cereals. There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by {{ill|Ono no Takamura|en|Ono no Takamura|WD=Q1139928|lt=Ono no Takamura}}, the {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=''kokushi''}} of {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Ōshū Province}},{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[:en:Fushimi Inari Shrine|Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}. The shrine is mentioned by the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} poet {{ill|Nōin|en|Nōin|WD=Q3074648|lt=Nōin}} during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Reizei|en|Emperor Go-Reizei|WD=Q356060|lt=Emperor Go-Reizei}} (1045-1068) and during the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} was awarded an estate by local warlord {{ill|Date Tanemune|en|Date Tanemune|WD=Q5227551|lt=Date Tanemune}}. It continued to be supported by the {{ill|Date clan|en|Date clan|WD=Q1151061|lt=Date clan}} of {{ill|Sendai Domain|en|Sendai Domain|WD=Q1062182|lt=Sendai Domain}} through the end of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. Following the start of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the shrine was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, {{ill|Date Yoshimura|en|Date Yoshimura|WD=Q116151|lt=Date Yoshimura}}, but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * {{ill|Karen Ann Smyers|en|Karen Ann Smyers|WD=Q6369485|lt=Smyers, Karen Ann}}. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] ==External links== *[http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288 Official site of the shrine] ==Notes== {{Commons Category|Takekoma-jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Izanami-firebirths}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]] {{Commons category|Takekoma-jinja}} [[fr:Takekoma Inari-jinja]] [[ja:竹駒神社]] [[uk:Святилище Такекома]] {{wikidata link|Q521319}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Takekoma-jinja]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[fr:Takekoma Inari-jinja]][[ja:竹駒神社]][[uk:Святилище Такекома]] [[co:commons:Category:Takekoma-jinja]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[fr:Takekoma Inari-jinja]][[ja:竹駒神社]][[uk:Святилище Такекома]] [[sn:commons:Category:Takekoma-jinja]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[fr:Takekoma Inari-jinja]][[ja:竹駒神社]][[uk:Святилище Такекома]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Takekoma Inari Jinja
竹駒稲荷神社 | image = File:Takekoma shrine.jpg | caption = Haiden of Takekoma Inari Jinja | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|38.105|N|140.862|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine}} | deity ={{ill|Ukanomitama|WD=Q3080728}} | founded_by = | established = 842 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Inari-chō, Iwanuma-shi, Miyagi-ken, | website = {{URL|1=http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288|2=www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Iwanuma|WD=Q518203}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Inari Shrine'''|竹駒稲荷神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the city of [[:en:Iwanuma, Miyagi|Iwanuma]] in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|WD=Q47896|lt=Miyagi Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It is considered one of the three main shrines dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari}}, and claims to be the second-oldest Inari shrine in Japan.Smyers, Karen Ann. The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press, 1999. 17-18. {{ISBN|0-8248-2102-5}} It was also referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Myojin'''|武隈明神}} Inari's traditional festival day is the first horse day (the sixth day) of the second month of the {{ill|en:Lunisolar calendar|en|en:Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=en:Lunisolar calendar}}; in recent years, the shrine has celebrated the event on a Sunday in February or early March. This festival is estimated to draw a quarter-million attendees.{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |title=Traditional Events |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Prefectural Government website |year=2004 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070220124655/http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |archivedate=2007-02-20 }} The shrine is also home to a horsemanship museum. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Takekoma Inari Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Ukanomitama]]| 倉稲魂神}}, traditionally identified as with Inari, and associated with agriculture, especially rice production. Secondary ''kami'' include {{nihongo|{{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi}}| 保食神}}, the goddess of food, and {{nihongo|[[Wakumusubi]]| 稚産霊神}}, the god of the five cereals. There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by {{ill|Ono no Takamura|en|Ono no Takamura|WD=Q1139928|lt=Ono no Takamura}}, the {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=''kokushi''}} of {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Ōshū Province}},{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[:en:Fushimi Inari Shrine|Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}. The shrine is mentioned by the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} poet {{ill|Nōin|en|Nōin|WD=Q3074648|lt=Nōin}} during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Reizei|en|Emperor Go-Reizei|WD=Q356060|lt=Emperor Go-Reizei}} (1045-1068) and during the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period}} was awarded an estate by local warlord {{ill|Date Tanemune|en|Date Tanemune|WD=Q5227551|lt=Date Tanemune}}. It continued to be supported by the {{ill|Date clan|en|Date clan|WD=Q1151061|lt=Date clan}} of {{ill|Sendai Domain|en|Sendai Domain|WD=Q1062182|lt=Sendai Domain}} through the end of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. Following the start of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, the shrine was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, {{ill|Date Yoshimura|en|Date Yoshimura|WD=Q116151|lt=Date Yoshimura}}, but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * {{ill|Karen Ann Smyers|en|Karen Ann Smyers|WD=Q6369485|lt=Smyers, Karen Ann}}. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] ==External links== *[http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288 Official site of the shrine] ==Notes== {{Commons Category|Takekoma-jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Izanami-firebirths}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]] {{Commons category|Takekoma-jinja}} [[fr:Takekoma Inari-jinja]] [[ja:竹駒神社]] [[uk:Святилище Такекома]] {{wikidata link|Q521319}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] ==External links== *[http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288 Official site of the shrine] ==Notes== {{Commons Category|Takekoma-jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Izanami-firebirths}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]] {{Commons category|Takekoma-jinja}} [[fr:Takekoma Inari-jinja]] [[ja:竹駒神社]] [[uk:Святилище Такекома]] {{wikidata link|Q521319}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

[[co:commons:Category:Takekoma-jinja]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[fr:Takekoma Inari-jinja]][[ja:竹駒神社]][[uk:Святилище Такекома]] [[co:commons:Category:Takekoma-jinja]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[fr:Takekoma Inari-jinja]][[ja:竹駒神社]][[uk:Святилище Такекома]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Takekoma-jinja]][[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]][[fr:Takekoma Inari-jinja]][[ja:竹駒神社]][[uk:Святилище Такекома]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Takekoma Inari Jinja
竹駒稲荷神社 | image = File:Takekoma shrine.jpg | caption = Haiden of Takekoma Inari Jinja | map_type = Japan Miyagi Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|38.105|N|140.862|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine|1=Inari shrine}} | deity ={{ill|Ukanomitama|WD=Q3080728|1=Ukanomitama}} | founded_by = | established = 842 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1 Inari-chō, Iwanuma-shi, Miyagi-ken, | website = {{URL|1=http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288|2=www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Iwanuma|WD=Q518203|1=Iwanuma}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Inari Shrine'''|竹駒稲荷神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} in the city of [[:en:Iwanuma, Miyagi|Iwanuma]] in {{ill|Miyagi Prefecture|en|Miyagi Prefecture|WD=Q47896|lt=Miyagi Prefecture|1=Miyagi Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It is considered one of the three main shrines dedicated to the ''[[kami]]'' {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari|1=Inari Ōkami}}, and claims to be the second-oldest Inari shrine in Japan.Smyers, Karen Ann. The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press, 1999. 17-18. {{ISBN|0-8248-2102-5}} It was also referred to as the {{nihongo|'''Takekoma Myojin'''|武隈明神}} Inari's traditional festival day is the first horse day (the sixth day) of the second month of the {{ill|en:Lunisolar calendar|en|en:Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=en:Lunisolar calendar|1=Lunisolar calendar}}; in recent years, the shrine has celebrated the event on a Sunday in February or early March. This festival is estimated to draw a quarter-million attendees.{{cite web |url=http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |title=Traditional Events |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Prefectural Government website |year=2004 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070220124655/http://www.pref.miyagi.jp/kankou/EN/Traditional_Events/Traditional_Events.htm |archivedate=2007-02-20 }} The shrine is also home to a horsemanship museum. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Takekoma Inari Shrine is {{nihongo|[[Ukanomitama]]| 倉稲魂神}}, traditionally identified as with Inari, and associated with agriculture, especially rice production. Secondary ''kami'' include {{nihongo|{{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ukemochi|1=Ukemochi}}| 保食神}}, the goddess of food, and {{nihongo|[[Wakumusubi]]| 稚産霊神}}, the god of the five cereals. There are also several smaller subsidiary shrines within the shrine grounds. ==History== The Takekoma Inari Shrine was established in 842 AD, reputedly by {{ill|Ono no Takamura|en|Ono no Takamura|WD=Q1139928|lt=Ono no Takamura|1=Ono no Takamura}}, the {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=''kokushi''|1=Kokushi (official)}} of {{ill|Mutsu Province|en|Mutsu Province|WD=Q907389|lt=Ōshū Province|1=Mutsu Province}},{{cite web |url=http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |title=Shrines/Temples: Takekoma Jinja |accessdate=2007-02-19 |work=Miyagi Tourist |year=2001 |url-status=dead |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20070929073237/http://www.kankou-miyagi.net/cgi-bin/ss_detail.cgi?ID=2140071&country=eng&ef=lst |archivedate=2007-09-29 }} as a branch of the famous [[:en:Fushimi Inari Shrine|Fushimi Inari Shrine]] south of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}. The shrine is mentioned by the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} poet {{ill|Nōin|en|Nōin|WD=Q3074648|lt=Nōin|1=Nōin}} during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Go-Reizei|en|Emperor Go-Reizei|WD=Q356060|lt=Emperor Go-Reizei|1=Emperor Go-Reizei}} (1045-1068) and during the {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}} was awarded an estate by local warlord {{ill|Date Tanemune|en|Date Tanemune|WD=Q5227551|lt=Date Tanemune|1=Date Tanemune}}. It continued to be supported by the {{ill|Date clan|en|Date clan|WD=Q1151061|lt=Date clan|1=Date clan}} of {{ill|Sendai Domain|en|Sendai Domain|WD=Q1062182|lt=Sendai Domain|1=Sendai Domain}} through the end of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. Following the start of {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the shrine was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. ==Notable structures== The ''Honden'' of the shrine was a structure built by the 5th ''daimyō'' of Sendai Domain, {{ill|Date Yoshimura|en|Date Yoshimura|WD=Q116151|lt=Date Yoshimura|1=Date Yoshimura}}, but burned down in a fire on November 21, 1990. It was rebuilt in 1994. Currently, the oldest structure in the shrine is the ''Zuishinmon'' two-story gate tower. It was built in 1812 and is a registered Important Cultural Property of Iwanuma City. ==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==References== * {{ill|Karen Ann Smyers|en|Karen Ann Smyers|WD=Q6369485|lt=Smyers, Karen Ann|1=Karen Ann Smyers}}. (1999). ''The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship.'' Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|9780824820589}}; {{ISBN|9780824821029}}; [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/039523475 OCLC 39523475] * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 3994492] ==External links== *[http://www.miyagi-jinjacho.or.jp/jinja-search/detail.php?code=310010288 Official site of the shrine] ==Notes== {{Commons Category|Takekoma-jinja}} {{Reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Izanami-firebirths}} {{Inari Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Takekoma Inari Shrine]] {{Commons category|Takekoma-jinja}} [[fr:Takekoma Inari-jinja]] [[ja:竹駒神社]] [[uk:Святилище Такекома]] {{wikidata link|Q521319}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Inari shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyagi Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]
